1f-cc4rDK6cVp_nXouloKB_KXpWMbKuWY:
Natan Dvir is an educator, artist, and editorial photographer whose work examines the human dimensions of cultural, social, and political issues. Since 2023, he has served as Managing Director of Education at the Houston Center for Photography, following his role establishing the Part-Time Programs at the International Center of Photography in New York. He holds an MBA from Tel Aviv University and an MFA in Photography from the School of Visual Arts. Israel's first Sony Imaging Ambassador, Dvir is represented by Polaris Images and Anastasia Photo.
His work has been exhibited internationally in museums, galleries, and festivals across the United States, Europe, South America, Asia, Australia, and Israel, including the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston, and the Triennial of Photography Hamburg. His photographs have appeared in National Geographic, The New York Times, New York Magazine, Der Spiegel, The Guardian, and Le Monde. He has received numerous international honors, including awards from Picture of the Year (POYi), PDN Photo Annual, American Photography, FotoFest Discoveries, and Critical Mass Top 50.

1K7dlWJT9zEfiEnfPKoXZfL6zUtqjRr6I: Camilo Gonzalez is a Colombian-born and Houston-based artist, organizer, and educator. Having studied photography and digital media at the University of Houston, his moving image works incorporate hand-drawn animation, assemblage, collage and are often site-specific and presented in public, accessible spaces. His work has been exhibited both individually and as a part of the collaborative Paradox in galleries, museums and film festivals throughout Texas and beyond including Lawndale Art Center, Rice University, Ann Arbor Film Festival, and CineMarfa. In his role as Education and Technical Director at Aurora Picture Show, he supports the visions of exhibiting artists and leads youth programs encouraging curiosity and experimentation in a variety of settings year-round.

John Bishop Fine Art www.johnbishopfineart info@johnbishopfineart.com Artist Statement As a painter, my work is deeply inﬂuenced by my background as a writer and librarian. In those roles, I developed 
a
 
deep
 
appreciation
 
for
 
language—the
 
way
 
it
 
shapes
 
meaning
 
and
 
communicates
 
complex
 
ideas.
 
I
 
learned
 
how
 
carefully
 
chosen
 
words
 
can
 
inform,
 
persuade,
 
and
 
evoke
 
emotions,
 
and
 
I
 
came
 
to
 
understand
 
language
 
as
 
both
 
symbolic
 
and
 
communicative.
 
 In my painting, I strive to employ a similar sensibility, but with a different vocabulary: one of color, line, and form. 
Just
 
as
 
a
 
well-crafted
 
poem
 
can
 
reveal
 
something
 
previously
 
unseen,
 
my
 
abstract
 
works
 
aim
 
to
 
create
 
visual
 
narratives
 
that
 
are
 
both
 
familiar
 
and
 
new,
 
offering
 
a
 
space
 
for
 
exploration
 
and
 
interpretation.
 
I
 
use
 
line,
 
color,
 
repetition,
 
and
 
symbolism
 
to
 
construct
 
a
 
visual
 
language
 
that
 
evokes
 
a
 
similar
 
emotional
 
resonance.
 
My
 
process
 
is
 
deeply
 
rooted
 
in
 
revision,
 
layering
 
elements
 
upon
 
elements,
 
often
 
hiding
 
them
 
from
 
view
 
in
 
the
 
ﬁnal
 
piece—yet
 
they
 
remain
 
discoverable
 
to
 
those
 
who
 
look
 
closely.
 
 It is through this iterative layering that the ﬁnal composition emerges, and only then do I know my role in the 
creation
 
is
 
ﬁnished.
 
From
 
that
 
point,
 
the
 
painting
 
transcends
 
my
 
intentions,
 
becoming
 
part
 
of
 
the
 
viewer’s
 
experience.
 
Their
 
personal
 
histories,
 
symbols,
 
and
 
beliefs
 
infuse
 
the
 
work
 
with
 
new
 
meanings,
 
allowing
 
each
 
observer
 
to
 
interpret
 
the
 
layers
 
in
 
their
 
own
 
unique
 
way.
 
This
 
dynamic
 
exchange
 
between
 
the
 
artwork
 
and
 
its
 
audience
 
is
 
what
 
makes
 
the
 
process
 
of
 
creation
 
so
 
exhilarating
 
for
 
me.
 
In
 
this
 
way,
 
my
 
abstract
 
expressionism
 
becomes
 
a
 
shared
 
journey,
 
inviting
 
others
 
to
 
engage
 
with
 
their
 
own
 
imagination
 
and
 
to
 
see
 
something
 
of
 
themselves
 
in
 
my
 
art.
 Bio 
John Bishop is an abstract expressionist painter who creates gestural mixed media work in acrylics, ink, 
collage,
 
pencil
 
and
 
marker.
 
His
 
work
 
centers
 
on
 
layering
 
color
 
and
 
mark
 
making
 
into
 
work
 
that
 
allows
 
a
 
different
 
experience
 
up
 
close
 
than
 
at
 
a
 
distance.
 
The
 
art
 
draws
 
one
 
deeper
 
into
 
discovery
 
and
 
recognition.
 
Though his passion for art began as a teenager, he was steered away from an artistic career by 
well-meaning
 
parents.
 
Instead,
 
he
 
became
 
an
 
English
 
teacher
 
and
 
later
 
a
 
librarian,
 
working
 
in
 
five
 
different
 
countries
 
over
 
the
 
course
 
of
 
three
 
decades.
 
Despite
 
this
 
detour,
 
in
 
2015
 
he
 
made
 
the
 
life-changing
 
decision
 
to
 
dedicate
 
himself
 
to
 
art
 
full
 
time.
 
Drawing on his experience as a creative writer, Bishop applies similar strategies in his painting—crafting 
layers
 
of
 
meaning
 
and
 
emotion
 
through
 
form,
 
color,
 
and
 
texture.
 
His
 
work
 
has
 
been
 
exhibited
 
locally
 
in
 
Houston,
 
across
 
the
 
U.S.,
 
and
 
internationally.
 
In
 
addition
 
to
 
his
 
artistic
 
practice,
 
Bishop
 
is
 
an
 
active
 
member
 
of
 
the
 
art
 
community
 
and
 
founded
 
Aripa
 
Arte,
 
a
 
nonprofit
 
organization
 
that
 
supports
 
Romanian
 
artists.
 
You can explore his work online at johnbishopfineart.com, or connect with him on Instagram and Facebook @johnbishopfineart. His studio is located in Houston, where he continues to create and engage with the 
local
 
and
 
international
 
art
 
scene.

1hjy_WcLqmJWRmdOLJTIlfOhWpS4VhXii:
Artist Statement 2026 
 
The objects I make are rooted in memory, history, form, function, and movement as it 
pertains to the human experience and the energies left lingering in the materials. The 
work translates into a language of landscapes, labor and love; it speaks of bodies 
rhythmically moving and laboring through time animated as the tools we use to help us 
work more efficiently and with purpose. My work is deeply interwoven with the historical 
significance of cotton, a fiber that holds great personal and familial meaning for me. 
Cotton represents the global and regional history of Tejano and Latino families, 
connecting them through generations of labor harvesting the plant in South Texas fields. 
Personally, cotton is present in generations of my family and part of my ancestral line. I 
come from a long line of cotton pickers, and their resilience is present in the work along 
with the quiet stillness and abundant sweetness of the South Texas monte.

1jHwkyVLvwLS2hEJcuYcreLvDItKctAST:
Top of Form
Roslyn M. Dupré
Bio

Through sculpture, bricolage, and textile craft, Roslyn M. Dupré assembles vernacular materials into forms that hover between artifact, proposition, and ruin. Her interdisciplinary background as a geoscientist, journalist, and editor informs an ongoing investigation into the material and symbolic architectures of contemporary life. Rooted in her cultural inheritance as a Louisiana Creole woman of Color from New Orleans, and shaped by a global trajectory spanning Japan, Norway, Egypt, Canada, and the United States, her work functions as an abstracted commentary on place-making, memory, and social mores. Dupré works across grids, geometries, and glyphs as she engages questions of material agency, cultural narrative, and borders between the latent and the manifest.
Dupré's recent exhibitions and residencies serve as physical coordinates for this practice. Her works emerge as provisional monuments, part excavation and part speculation, and have been featured in solo exhibitions including The daily devotional at Lawndale Art Space and On U.S. at LRT Gallery, as well as the Texas Biennial presentationThe Big Sky: Thermals and Thresholds. Her bronze foundry work is on view at the City of Houston’s Civic Art Collection at Hobby Airport and her textiles have been shown as part of Prolog at the Untitled Art Fair. In February, she completed a residency at the Houston Center for Contemporary Craft and she is currently in an artist-in-residence at PAC Art and a member of the inaugural round of the Rick Lowe Studio Residency.
1xnziOqHMc7EFiyWnvwqomwGL9MCSSJAW:
www.zulmavega.com
Artist Statement
Zulma Vega
In life, there are pivotal moments when the present becomes a mirror, 
reﬂecting the paths we’ve traveled and prompting us to consider how 
our experiences shape our futures. This realization ignited in me a 
desire to surrender control and embrace the unpredictability of 
material exploration and viewer engagement through my sculptures and 
installations.
My work is deeply rooted in the land—drawing from my origins, my 
journeys, and my current environment—and emphasizes the 
interconnectedness of all beings. I engage in relational sculpture, 
blending materials such as concrete, coffee sacks, and jute/ﬁque twine 
with metaphorical narratives inspired by the natural world and ancestral 
cultures. These materials invite tactile interaction, challenging 
conventional boundaries and encouraging viewers to engage with the 
work through touch and physical presence.
I aspire to create 'open artworks'—pieces that transcend mere visual 
appreciation, inviting sensory and emotional involvement that allows the 
viewer to complete the experience. My aim is to dissolve perceived 
boundaries, fostering a deeper understanding of our identities as 
individuals, our connections within communities, and our relationship to 
the natural world and humanity as a whole.
1w2nX04DaB8m-kM8YEd1Aj8cBH-RHClEZ:
Tatiana Escallón B. 
 
 
 
Artistic Biography 
Born and raised in Bogotá, Colombia, where she completed her artistic education before 
emigrating to the United States in 2000, Tatiana Escallón is currently based in Houston, TX. 
Her current abstract practice explores displacement, mobility, and the shifting boundaries 
of time and space as lived conditions of contemporary existence. Working across painting, 
sculpture, and textile-based works, she investigates movement not as metaphor but as 
structure. 
She holds a B.A. in Graphic Design from Universidad Jorge Tadeo Lozano (Bogotá) and is 
currently pursuing a Master’s degree in Philosophy for Contemporary Challenges at 
Universitat Oberta de Catalunya (UOC), integrating material experimentation with critical 
inquiry. 
Her recent solo exhibitions include Mapping Time at The Nave Museum (Victoria, TX) and 
The I in Immigration at Anya Tish Gallery (Houston, TX). She has participated in the WTA X 
Biennale (Women in Textile Art, Manizales, Colombia), exhibited in Bogota at Galería Otros 
360, and presented work at Project Row Houses (Houston, TX). 
Tatiana Escallón has completed residencies at PAC Houston and Asia Society Center Texas 
and is a recipient of the SACI Grant from the City of Houston through Houston Arts Alliance.
1x7FVHKafCXETeqRaCrlVvB7W9na25l2T:
Biography

Lydia Bodnar-Balahutrak is a Houston-based visual artist whose multi-faceted work combines text and image. Her artworks are layered mixed-media documentations of past and current events, filtered through her cultural, social, and political frames of reference.

Lydia Bodnar-Balahutrak completed her undergraduate art education at Kent State University, studied at the Corcoran School of Art and received her Master of Fine Arts degree in painting from George Washington University, Washington, D.C. Born and raised in Cleveland, Ohio, she moved to Houston in 1977, where she continues to live and work. 

In 1991, an IREX grant enabled her to travel to Ukraine, her ancestral homeland, for the first time. The trip opened her eyes and soul to a long-suffering yet hopeful people, to a land beautiful but ravaged by the genocidal soviet regime. This marked a pivotal turning point in her work and world view. She resolved to commit her creative efforts to “bear witness” to crucial moments in history, especially those that are little-known, suppressed or distorted with lies. In 1996, a visit to the Chornobyl Zone left her with a lasting impression of nature’s healing power and reclamation of life. Fauna and flora, symbolic of resilience, grace and hope, became recurring motifs. She continues to explore narrative with collage, text, and figuration. 

The artist has lectured and taught studio drawing and painting at the University of Houston and the Glassell School of Art, Museum of Fine Arts. She has participated in national and international exhibitions, and been awarded artist residencies in France, Ukraine, and US venues. A monograph about her art was published in 2005, a second edition in 2026. Her artwork has been acquired for private and public collections including The Museum of South Texas, Oxford University, and, in 2021, the Houston Civic Art Collection for permanent display at Hobby Airport. She was awarded a 2022-23 Fulbright U.S. Scholar fellowship to study contemporary issues-oriented artmaking in Ukraine. Due to the war, her project was re-assigned to Poland, where she worked with young displaced Ukrainian artists and curated a show of their work at the University of the Arts in Poznan. On her return to the USA in 2024, Texas A & M University’s Wright Gallery hosted a solo exhibition featuring her large scroll-like Pandemic Lamentations series, documenting the COVID-19 pandemic. In 2025 her solo exhibition at the Hooks-Epstein Galleries in Houston was titled Turning Toward the Light. It was a tribute to Ukraine’s courageous fight for freedom.

www.LydiaBodnarBalahutrak.com
1qVVVoiinauUV0EssOGk9nDELLQGJ3-Wj: Doug Welsh Artist Statement Navigating the world with music as my companion, I listen to one song on loop until a painting is complete. My ongoing body of work – Life Raft – is ritualistic and anchoring. I embrace a philosophy of stability and harmony in my life, my practice, and as a guiding compositional strategy within the paintings themselves. The color and spatial relationships in my paintings can be harmonious, tenuous, or tumultuous, depending on my state of mind. Ultimately, I seek balance in the interaction of these elements within my paintings, and within myself. Life Raft is what keeps me aﬂoat. Art Fairs 2025 F (solo booth), Untitled Art, Houston, TX 2024 F (group booth, with blvxmth and Jen Fisher), Independent Art Fair, NYC, NY Solo Exhibitions 2025 Lifelike, F , Houston, TX 2024 re-rite, Art League Houston, Houston, TX 2021 Life Raft (curated by Zoie Buske), Elgin Street Studios, Houston, TX Selected Group Exhibitions 2026 All Kinds of Love (with Sarah Fisher and Shang-Yi Hua), Houston, TX 2025 BLOCK 10: Celebrating a Decade of the BLOCK Program, Houston, TX 2024 Form + Void, LSC North Harris Fine Arts Gallery, Houston, TX 2023 Ignition, The Jung Center, Houston, TX 2023 Shifting Systems (with Garrett Gribin), Jardín, Houston, TX 2023 HARRY SMITH’S SHIRT, F , Houston, TX 2023 Before Apollo, Before the Sun, Galveston Artist Residency, Galveston, TX 2022 44th Thesis Exhibition, Blaber Art Museum, Houston, TX Curatorial Work 2025 a way to mend, Blaber Art Museum, Houston, TX 2024 leftovers, landSPACE: a kunsthalle, Austin, TX 2024 It’s OK to Feel This, LAR Gallery, Houston, TX 2024 orbit, Elgin Street Studios Gallery 1 Education 2022 University of Houston (Houston, TX) - MFA in Painting 2014 Bates College (Lewiston, ME) - BA in Visual Arts
1eApmqzMhIuW0aegcaks0depqnDHynlA3:
Born in Argentina, Gladys Poorte spent her childhood in Buenos Aires. Her family lived and traveled extensively abroad, immersing her in diverse cultures and shaping a global outlook that continues to inform her work today.
At 25, Gladys moved to the United States to pursue a Master's in Education at Penn State University. She later returned to school to study Studio Art at the University of Texas at Austin, and has worked as a full-time artist ever since. She has called Houston home since 2020.
A multidisciplinary artist, Gladys works across 3D installations, paintings, drawings, sculptures, and photography. Her process begins with building 3D installations from a wide range of materials and found objects. These installations function as a kind of stage where narratives unfold—serving as the source material for work in other media, including painting, drawing, and photography.
Her work draws on a range of themes, including the human condition, the perils currently facing humanity, and the future path society may take. She is also drawn to the idea of longing for a magical or mysterious place in a world where everything seems to have already been explored, photographed, and posted online.
Gladys's work has been shown in numerous solo and group exhibitions at Davis Gallery in Austin, TX, where she is represented. Her 2024 solo show was featured at Glasstire among the top 5 shows in Texas. 
Her work has also appeared at Austin City Hall, the 2009 Texas Biennial, and various juried exhibitions, as well as at the Fort Worth Community Arts Center and the Abilene Center for Contemporary Arts. In Houston, her work has been featured at Redbud Arts Center, in a juried ALMAAHH exhibition, and at Tank Space. It is also part of the City of Houston's permanent airport art collection.
1Q_kKx7y1vZlAAWqLsI_M8qrJPG5RXCsE:
Bogdan Mihai
936.668.0109
info@bogdanfotoart.com
www.bogdanfotoart.com
Bogdan Mihai is a US–Romanian visual artist based in Houston, Texas. His 
practice spans photography, abstract mixed media, and painting, with a 
focus on the interplay between form, imagination, and cultural narratives. 
Working from his studio at Silver Street Studios, Bogdan develops bodies 
of work that often bring together fragments of urban life, personal memory, 
and global perspectives, inviting viewers to explore layered visual stories 
open to multiple interpretations.
Since transitioning from a career in television and international media, 
Bogdan has exhibited his work in the United States, Romania, Argentina, 
Hungary, and Mexico. His artistic path reﬂects both his international 
experiences and his commitment to building connections within the 
communities where he works and shows his art.
In addition to his studio practice, Bogdan is the co-founder of Aripa Arte, an 
initiative dedicated to supporting Romanian artists in both their creative 
growth and professional development. Through his work as an artist, 
curator, and advocate, he seeks to foster meaningful dialogue between 
artists and audiences, extending the reach of contemporary art across 
borders.
1zZriUZSP2BgbNPpVFgdLDff6isquqMKG:
Biography 
 
 I am a figurative artist that has been living in the Galveston area for the past 20 years. I moved here in 
2005 after living in Ireland, England, Taiwan and Korea. Much of my childhood was spent in the United 
States, and I was born here. However, my Mother is American and my Father is Irish. They had difficulty 
planting their roots in one place, so we moved every 3-4 years. I competed my BFA in Painting at Dublin, 
Ireland’s National College of Art and Design and lived there several more years occupying studios at 
Temple Bar Gallery and Studios and Phoenix Studios. My early work responded to my youth growing up 
in Asia. I, then, transitioned almost completely into portraiture and figurative painting. My BFA program 
really discouraged painting, so much of my undergrad work was drawing. After finishing my BFA, I 
realized how much I loved to paint and decided to experiment and learn as much as I could about 
painting by myself. I’ve explored numerous styles of paint application from pointillism, expressionism, 
cubist type work, works on paper, life painting, oils, acrylic, and more. I try to continually learn and 
improve, so my application of paint seems to evolve with that desire. In 2014, I moved to New York City 
for three years and obtained my MFA in Painting from the New York Academy of Art. I loved the 
programs. They offered extensive classical training in anatomy, life drawing, painting, art history, 
contemporary art studies, composition, portraiture and more. It was really the place for me I felt. I 
received a lot of training that I wasn’t able to obtain on my own. I was even awarded a residency to 
Russia. I left New York, in 2017, and moved back to the Galveston area. I have since lectured and 
exhibited throughout Houston, Galveston and surrounding areas.
1Dy879YohshTgIuNMIo9v5ul3ek9-DSQ7:
Mi trabajo es una mezcla de mi pasión por el comportamiento humano y la necesidad de expresar a gritos mis sentimientos.
Una obra llena de color donde materializó lo que  es para mi vivir contento siendo compasivo, amando, sirviendo y agradeciendo profundamente al que nos permite estar.
El darle forma, textura y nombre a mis sentimientos ha sido mi mejor terapia.
1XblnJ_BEzDv5S1GxbQdHVn85fNaqBD2t:
Artist Biography for Artists for Venezuela 

Garrett Griffin was born into a military family and had a peripatetic childhood, moving to several places internationally and within the United States. These experiences in various cultures and environments gave him a strong interest in how societies and the natural world mesh together. He creates bodily forms that meld succinctly into exhibition spaces. Using site specific installation, Griffin is commenting on our bodies, architecture and the natural world. He received his BA in Studio Arts from the University of Texas at Arlington in 2013 and his MFA at University of Houston in 2024. He has had solo shows at Front Gallery and LAR gallery as well as participated in group exhibitions at Presa House, The Court at TCC, Lawndale Art Center, Community Artist Collective, Barbara Davis Gallery, The Painting Center and the Blaffer Museum. Griffin has also attended residencies at the Vermont Studio Center and Marfa Dust.
1DIgfwyOiniXSCEOcRgAGyNZGxpNp30ox:
Artist: Miguel Ángel Ávila Artists for Venezuela 2026
 
About 
Miguel Ángel Ávila is a Mexican artist based in Texas. He’s been a resident of 
Southwest Houston for almost two decades. Working mostly with acrylics and 
graphite, his ﬁgurative drawings and paintings are inspired by his cultural background, 
dreamscapes, memories, color, his formal training (architecture), and his experience of 
growing up in a modern and vibrant metropolis in northern Mexico .
Avila’s artwork has been exhibited and collected in North America and Europe.
1atcqJVXjpO8CsE_bVjfMdb2Tc65rFEMZ:
Biography 
 
 I am a practicing artist, musician, teacher and story teller that has been creating 
art since childhood. I spent many of my early years, before obtaining my degrees, 
raising a family, creating portraits, and teaching art in my children’s schools. At 
the moment, I am an activities director and art therapist at a local Dickinson 
home for the elderly. 
 I spent my childhood growing up in the Midwestern United States. I met my 
Irish husband in my twenties and had the opportunity to live all over the world. I 
have lived on 5 continents and numerous countries (Ireland, England, Venezuela, 
Taiwan, Korea, South Africa and the US) and states. I bring my love for art to 
create and teach classes wherever the road takes me. 
 In 2002, in my fifties, I completed my BFA at Dublin, Ireland’s National College of 
Art and Design. I was, also, awarded a residency, during that time at The Lyme 
Academy. I went on to complete my MFA, in 2004, in London, at University of 
East London. Upon moving to Texas, in 2005, I have taught at public schools as 
well as local colleges including: Brazosport College and Lee College. I, also, 
taught at The Ronald McDonald House through the Galveston Art Center.
1g-VMNhPZMNo0e0emUws8cgQTTF-XTNso:
Francisco Catalano B

Caracas, 1986. Poet, performer, artist, cultural promoter. He published "l" (2010), the title of his first book and of his entire poetic work; "Libro 2" (2016), winner of the XXI Fernando Paz Castillo Poetry Prize along with "Libro 3" (2017); he published Cantocuarto libro 4 (2022) with LP5 editora, and "Quinto Libro" (El Taller Blanco Ediciones, 2025). He is currently working on an audiovisual revisiting of his published work in the form of a TikTok account called "ProyectoLibroSexto," and is writing the trilogy "El Séptimo," titled "Casi un Libro," published by Eloísa Cartonera (2025). He has developed a multimedia poetry recital project called REVITAL. He won the Intermedial Literature award at the 5th ULA Art Biennial for Revital Libro de los Prólogos I. He is a producer of the audiovisual poetry project Página=Pantalla and of the podcast Píldora de Poesía on Spotify. He recently founded "El bebé literario," an audiovisual literature magazine. He lives in Buenos Aires.
1xUJeztt1Dq9ozlXXTDVqPC9Z3UCL7AKY: ‏Diana-Sofia Estrada is a visual artist/educator born and raised in Houston, Texas. Estrada’s work encompasses paintings, installations, social practice, video, and animation to question our immediate visual culture and environment. Drawing upon her family's Guatemalan heritage, her current work investigates broken familial narratives and visual/atmospheric conditions. She received her BFA in Painting/Drawing and BA in Psychology from U.N.T. and her MFA in Art from the California Institute of the Arts. Estrada has exhibited and screened her work nationally and internationally including Alice Yard Space (Trinidad), L.A.C.E. Exhibitions (Los Angeles, CA), at Artlab at the Smithsonian Hirschhorn Museum ( Washington D.C.), Matucana 100  (Santiago, Chile), Children's Museum of  the Arts( New York, NY), Torrance Art Museum (Torrance, CA), and at Wonzimer Gallery (Los Angeles, CA), and with Marta Lidia Umaña-Matta de Wer at San Jacinto College Central Gallery, Pasadena, TX, 2026.  Estrada is a member of the artist-run gallery Throughline.
1wOM5EiETlkdm2fun-i31muZ79kC07tNs:
Isela Aguirre is a multi-disciplinary artist based in Houston, Texas. She uses photography, camera-less photography processes, collage, fiber, painting, and installation in her work. Her work is bold, colorful, and playful with an air of the ephemeral and non-precious. Isela is inspired by the idea that we as individuals are in a constant state of change whether we decide to embrace it or not. She uses nature and her immediate surroundings as her muse. The images she captures are transformed into digital collages, fiber constructions and site specific installations.
Isela Aguirre is a native Houstonian. She received her BFA in Studio Photography and her MFA in Painting from the University of Houston. She has received grants to create public installations and has shown her work in solo and group shows in Houston and Austin.
1qwvI-pgRoFN3IJhKMhzAFVnlz1RctqzZ:
Ellen H Ray
www.EllenHRay.com
ehray.art@gmail.com
Ellen H Ray is an interdisciplinary artist whose practice is rooted in meditation and the 
study of physical and spiritual connections across life forms. Her work reﬂects her belief 
that the Earth, and the universe encompassing it, is a master plan of intelligent design 
that holds lessons for us about resource sharing, communication, and systems that 
work in community. She makes drawings, paintings, objects, and installations in which 
organic and spiritual imagery mix with vibration patterns and other visuals she 
experiences in her meditation. She is a member of Throughline Collective here in 
Houston, has shown with many galleries and learning institutions including Monterroso 
Gallery, Ardest Gallery, Bosque Gallery, Marie Flickenger Fine Arts Center, Dallas Bath 
House Cultural Center, Throughline Collective, Mönchskirche Cultural Center, 
Salzwedel Germany. Her work is in public and private collections throughout Texas and 
beyond.
